title: The End of the Tunnel — Case File 1: Peacock Alley
description: Case File 1 — Training Detective: The first chapter of the larger Tunnel story. Players solve the historian’s murder and discover the tunnel has been used recently.

answerKey.method: The poisoned champagne flute switched during the Peacock Alley toast
answerKey.motive: To hide that she forged a Grosjean letter and sold it as authentic
answerKey.keyEvidence: lipstick on the wrong flute, the stage cue card, and the torn auction receipt
answerKey.explanation: Celeste Moreau is responsible. lipstick on the wrong flute, the stage cue card, and the torn auction receipt proves the method and timeline. This chapter reveals: The historic rumor was used to hide a present-day crime inside the tunnel.

publicClues.title: Case File 1: Peacock Alley
publicClues.text: Case File 1: Peacock Alley begins inside The Tunnel. This is one chapter in a larger story, not a separate remake of the same mystery.

publicClues.title: The New Death
publicClues.text: The victim is Dr. Malcolm Vire. The old Peacock Alley legend still matters, but this chapter adds a deeper layer to the ongoing investigation.

phone.title: Phone Evidence 1
phone.text: Malcolm missed two calls from Celeste before the lights went out.

phone.title: Phone Evidence 2
phone.text: An eleven-second call connected as Malcolm moved toward the King Hotel door.

messages.title: Messages Evidence 1
messages.text: Malcolm texted: “The letter is not old. I can prove it.”

messages.title: Messages Evidence 2
messages.text: A reply from Celeste said: “Not in the lobby. Bring the ledger below.”

maps.title: Maps Evidence 1
maps.text: Malcolm’s phone pinged near the Heidelberg service stairs at 8:07 PM.

maps.title: Maps Evidence 2
maps.text: Celeste’s device stayed close to the stage curtain during the blackout window.

bank.title: Bank Evidence 1
bank.text: A private payment references “Peacock Alley restoration materials” but posts to a performer vendor alias.

bank.title: Bank Evidence 2
bank.text: The payout trail points back to Celeste through an auction deposit.

photos.title: Photos Evidence 1
photos.text: A guest photo shows Malcolm holding the wrong champagne flute.

photos.title: Photos Evidence 2
photos.text: Another photo captures a stage cue card placed beside Celeste’s microphone stand.

social.title: Social Evidence 1
social.text: A teaser video about the old hotel rumor was posted minutes before the murder.

social.title: Social Evidence 2
social.text: The reposted clip cuts out the seconds showing Celeste cross behind the bar curtain.

contacts.title: Contacts Evidence 2
contacts.text: A second contact note says: “Ask about King Hotel key stamp.”

notes.title: Notes Evidence 1
notes.text: Detective note: the killer needed a public distraction and a private motive.

notes.title: Notes Evidence 2
notes.text: Detective note: the rumor is useful only because it makes everyone stop looking at the glass.

files.title: Files Evidence 1
files.text: The security export skips the exact Peacock Alley blackout window.

files.title: Files Evidence 2
files.text: File metadata shows the export was edited after the preview guest list was finalized.

browser.title: Browser Evidence 1
browser.text: A browser search asks how to age paper and remove modern ink fluorescence.

browser.title: Browser Evidence 2
browser.text: Another search matches Celeste’s cover story: “Huey Long tunnel mistress rumor Heidelberg King Hotel.”

rounds.title: Final Accusation

rounds.dialogue: Final accusation is open. Name the culprit, method, motive, evidence, and larger truth.

openingNarration: Detectives, welcome to Case File 1: Peacock Alley. The Tunnel has reopened its legend for one night, and historian Dr. Malcolm Vire is dead near the restricted door. Start with the room, the blackout, and the glass. The old story is loud, but the first truth is physical.
revealScript: Celeste Moreau killed Dr. Malcolm Vire to protect a forged letter she sold as authentic. The Huey Long rumor pulled attention toward the past, but the evidence proved a modern fraud. The final discovery matters most: the sealed passage has been opened recently.

questions.prompt: Checkpoint 1: What makes Malcolm’s death suspicious?

options.label: A detail that sounds dramatic but does not prove the case
The body was found at a restricted tunnel door during a staged blackout
A rumor from the old hotel story with no physical support
A coincidence that does not affect the timeline

questions.prompt: Checkpoint 2: Which timeline clue matters most?

options.label: A detail that sounds dramatic but does not prove the case
Celeste could reach the toast table and stage curtain during the blackout
A rumor from the old hotel story with no physical support
A coincidence that does not affect the timeline

questions.prompt: Checkpoint 3: Which item breaks Celeste’s story?

options.label: A detail that sounds dramatic but does not prove the case
The lipstick mark sits on the flute Malcolm should not have held
A rumor from the old hotel story with no physical support
A coincidence that does not affect the timeline

questions.prompt: Checkpoint 4: What was the real motive in this chapter?

options.label: A detail that sounds dramatic but does not prove the case
Celeste needed Malcolm quiet before he exposed the forged letter
A rumor from the old hotel story with no physical support
A coincidence that does not affect the timeline

questions.prompt: Checkpoint 5: What larger story opens after the first solve?

options.label: A detail that sounds dramatic but does not prove the case
The tunnel was opened recently, so this is bigger than one murder
A rumor from the old hotel story with no physical support
A coincidence that does not affect the timeline
A person tied only to the old rumor
Celeste Moreau
A guest with no access evidence
A victim from an earlier case file

questions.prompt: Final Accusation: What method was used?

options.label: A random accident during the crowd movement
The poisoned champagne flute switched during the Peacock Alley toast
A fight outside the hotel lobby
A ghost-story scare that went wrong

questions.prompt: Final Accusation: What was the motive?

options.label: To cancel the speakeasy event
To hide that she forged a Grosjean letter and sold it as authentic
To steal an ordinary bar tab
To prove Peacock Alley never existed

questions.prompt: Final Accusation: Which evidence combination proves it?

options.label: A cocktail umbrella, a playlist, and a menu typo
lipstick on the wrong flute, the stage cue card, and the torn auction receipt
A parking ticket, a rumor, and a receipt
A song request, a cracked glass, and a selfie

questions.prompt: Final Accusation: What larger truth does this chapter reveal?

options.label: The case is only about a confirmed historic romance
The historic rumor was used to hide a present-day crime inside the tunnel
No one has used the passage in modern times
The murder is unrelated to The Tunnel
